9.text: Standard library math for the sqrt function used to scale attention scores by the head dimension.

10.text: Import os for building portable file paths to the dataset.

11.text: Import random for shuffling the dataset and seeding reproducibility.

12.text: Wall-clock timing for the FP32 vs INT8 benchmark.

14.text: Import the core PyTorch library for tensor operations and autograd.

15.text: Imports PyTorch's neural network module, which provides layers like Linear and Embedding that we'll later quantize to INT8.

16.text: Import PyTorch's functional API for operations like softmax, cross-entropy, and relu.

18.text: Fix both the Python and PyTorch random seeds so results are reproducible across runs.

19.text: Set the PyTorch random seed for reproducible weight initialization and sampling.

22.text: Build a portable file path to the dataset, relative to this script's location.

23.text: Download the dataset from the internet if it is not already on disk.

29.text: Read all names from the dataset file, one per line, stripping whitespace.

30.text: Randomize the training order so the model does not memorize sequences of names.

34.text: Build a sorted list of unique characters in the dataset to form the token vocabulary.

35.text: Use the length of the character set as the beginning-of-sequence token ID.

36.text: The full vocabulary has 27 tokens: 26 lowercase letters plus the BOS marker.

40.text: Larger model than lab 03 (64 vs 16 embedding dim) to make quantization effects visible. At 16 dimensions the savings are tiny; at 64 the 4x compression is meaningful.

43.text: Names rarely exceed 16 characters, so this caps the positional embedding table and keeps sequences compact.

44.text: Each attention head operates on a 16-dimensional slice of the 64-dimensional embedding.

47.text: Root-mean-square normalization that stabilizes activations without learned parameters.

53.text: Scale the input by its inverse root-mean-square, ensuring activations have roughly unit magnitude without learned parameters.

56.text: Standard causal attention, same as lab 03.

59.text: Q, K, V, output projections. These are the weights that will be quantized to INT8.

60.text: Linear projection that transforms embeddings into key vectors for attention.

61.text: Linear projection that transforms embeddings into value vectors for attention.

62.text: Project the concatenated multi-head output back to the model dimension.

64.text: Run the attention computation: project to Q, K, V, apply the causal mask, and combine.

65.text: Extract batch size, sequence length, and embedding dimension from the input tensor shape.

66.text: Project and reshape the input into query vectors, one per attention head.

67.text: Project and reshape the input into key vectors, one per attention head.

68.text: Project and reshape the input into value vectors, one per attention head.

70.text: Compute Q dot K transposed, scaled by sqrt(head_dim). The scaling prevents softmax from saturating as dimensionality grows.

71.text: Build an upper-triangular boolean mask so each token can only attend to itself and earlier positions.

72.text: Set all future positions to negative infinity so softmax assigns them zero weight, enforcing the causal left-to-right constraint.

73.text: Convert attention scores into a probability distribution that sums to one over the key dimension.

75.text: Multiply attention weights by value vectors and reshape. Each position now holds a context-aware blend of information from earlier tokens.

76.text: Project the concatenated multi-head output back to the model dimension through a linear layer.

79.text: A standard two-layer feed-forward network with ReLU activation in between.

83.text: Compress the expanded 256-dimensional representation back to the 64-dimensional embedding.

85.text: Run the input through the two-layer feed-forward network with ReLU activation.

86.text: The complete feed-forward computation: expand to 4x width, apply ReLU nonlinearity, and compress back in a single expression.

89.text: One transformer block containing self-attention and a feed-forward network with residual connections.

92.text: Normalize activations before the attention sublayer to stabilize training gradients.

93.text: The causal self-attention sublayer that lets each token attend to previous positions.

94.text: Normalize activations before the MLP sublayer to stabilize training gradients.

95.text: The feed-forward network that processes each token independently after attention has mixed information across positions.

97.text: Apply attention and MLP sublayers with residual connections that let gradients flow easily.

98.text: Add the attention output to the residual stream, preserving the original information.

99.text: Add the MLP output to the residual stream so the model can refine representations incrementally.

103.text: Full model, same architecture as lab 03 but larger (64-dim instead of 16-dim).

106.text: Look up learned token embeddings in a 27 by 64 table, one vector per token.

107.text: Look up learned position embeddings in a 16 by 64 table, one vector per position.

108.text: Normalize the combined token-plus-position embedding before feeding it into the transformer.

109.text: A stack of transformer blocks, each containing attention and MLP sublayers.

111.text: Apply the custom weight initialization function to every module in the model.

115.text: Draw initial weights from a narrow Gaussian so activations stay reasonably scaled at the start.

118.text: Run the full model on the input sequence to produce next-token logits for every position.

119.text: Extract the batch size and sequence length from the input tensor shape.

120.text: Look up a learned embedding vector for each input token to represent its identity.

121.text: Create a position embedding for each slot in the sequence so the model knows token ordering.

122.text: Add token and position embeddings together, then normalize before entering the transformer.

123.text: Pass the normalized representation through each transformer block in sequence.

125.text: Map the final hidden states to vocabulary-sized logits for predicting the next token.

129.text: Use CPU for this tiny model since it trains in seconds without a GPU.

130.text: Instantiate the model and move it to the target device for training.

131.text: Show parameter count, larger than lab 03 to make quantization meaningful.

133.text: Set up the Adam optimizer with momentum and epsilon tuned for this small model.

134.text: Train for 1000 steps, enough for this model to learn basic character-level name patterns.

136.text: Standard training loop, identical to other labs.

137.text: Pick the next training document by cycling through the shuffled name list.

138.text: Encode each character in the name as a token ID, bookended by BOS markers.

139.text: Limit the sequence length to block_size so it fits the model's positional embedding table.

141.text: Convert the first n token IDs into a tensor the model can consume as input.

142.text: Build the target sequence by shifting the input one position to the right for next-token prediction.

144.text: Run the full model on the current input batch to get next-token predictions.

145.text: Measure how far the model's predictions are from the true next tokens using cross-entropy loss.

147.text: Clear out any leftover gradients from the previous step so they do not accumulate.

148.text: Compute gradients for every parameter by propagating the loss backward through the graph.

149.text: Linearly reduce the learning rate from its initial value down to zero over the course of training.

150.text: Write the decayed learning rate into every parameter group so the optimizer uses it on the next step.

152.text: Apply the optimizer step, adjusting every parameter in the direction that reduces the loss.

163.text: The quantized linear layer: stores weights as INT8 integers (1 byte each) instead of FP32 floats (4 bytes each). A single scale factor maps between the two representations.

164.text: Takes a trained FP32 linear layer and quantizes its weights.

166.text: Detach the FP32 weight tensor from the computation graph since we only need the raw numbers for the quantization math.

167.text: Compute the scale factor: map the weight range [-max, +max] to [-127, +127]. This single scalar is the key, it determines the precision of the quantization.

168.text: Round to nearest integer and cast to INT8. Each weight now fits in 1 byte instead of 4. The register_buffer saves it as a non-parameter tensor (no gradients).

169.text: Keep the scale factor as a buffer so we can undo the quantization during inference by multiplying INT8 values back to float.

170.text: Carry over bias if the original layer had one (ours don't).

172.text: Forward: dequantize on-the-fly by casting back to float and multiplying by scale. In production, INT8 matmul kernels avoid this cast entirely, they compute directly in integer arithmetic.

173.text: Cast INT8 to float, multiply by scale, then do the matrix multiply. Mathematically: x @ (W_int8 * scale)^T. The approximation error is small because neural network weights are typically well-distributed.

176.text: Quantize the entire model: create a fresh copy and replace all Linear layers with QuantizedLinear.

177.text: Instantiate a fresh model with the same architecture. We'll load trained weights into it and then swap its Linear layers for quantized ones.

178.text: Copy the trained FP32 weights into the new model so we preserve learned behavior before replacing layers with quantized versions.

179.text: Walk every module in the model.

180.text: Look for Linear layers to quantize.

181.text: Found one, replace with quantized version.

189.text: Controls how peaked the sampling distribution is. Lower temperature means more deterministic output; higher means more creative variety.

192.text: Decorator: no gradient tracking needed for inference.

193.text: Generate names, same function for both FP32 and INT8 models.

194.text: Switch to evaluation mode so the model produces consistent outputs during generation.

196.text: Loop through each sample, generating one name at a time from the model.

197.text: Begin each generated name with the beginning-of-sequence token so the model starts fresh.

199.text: Feed the current token sequence through the model to get next-token logits, keeping only the last block_size tokens.

200.text: Temperature-scaled softmax and sample in one line.

201.text: Randomly draw one token from the probability distribution for stochastic generation.

202.text: If the model predicts BOS, treat it as an end-of-name signal and stop generating.

204.text: Add the newly sampled token to the growing sequence and continue generating the next one.

209.text: Benchmark: measure model size on disk and generation speed.

210.text: Temp file import for saving the model.

212.text: Write the model's state dict to a temporary file on disk so we can measure its serialized size in bytes.

214.text: Serialize the state dict, this is what you'd ship to production.

215.text: File size in MB, INT8 should be roughly 4x smaller.

216.text: Time 100 generations for a stable measurement.

217.text: Generate 100 names in a batch to get a stable timing measurement for the benchmark.

218.text: Package the file size in MB and average time per generation into a tuple for the benchmark comparison table.

224.text: Quantize the trained model to INT8.

225.text: Run the same benchmark suite on the INT8 model to compare size and speed against the original FP32 baseline.

232.text: Side-by-side comparison: generate 10 names from each model. Quality should be nearly identical despite 4x compression.

235.text: Decode and print each generated name.
